pitch.heading: Why Jurepi?
pitch.intro: Jurepi is a free collection of the small tools you reach for every day — calculators and converters, text and developer utilities, random pickers and games — all in one place. There's nothing to install and no account to create; each tool runs right in your browser. Open it when you need it, close it when you're done.

items.title: Instant, no sign-up
items.description: No login, no install, no payment. Open the link and the tool runs immediately, with every feature free to use.

items.title: Safe in your browser
items.description: Whatever you type is processed inside your own browser. Nothing is sent to a server or stored remotely, so your data never leaves your device.

items.title: Grows by request
items.description: Ask for a tool or feature and it gets built and added, one at a time. Only tools born from real use make it in.

pitch.categoriesBody: You'll find calculators (age, unit, and lunar-calendar conversion), text tools (character counting, find & replace), converter and developer tools (URL, Base64, JSON, QR, JWT), random pickers (ladder game, roulette), journaling and bookmarks, plus fun and news/ranking tools. Search the list below or browse by category to get started.

ladder.title: Ladder Game
ladder.description: Climb down the ladder and get your prize. A fair lottery game.
ladder.lead: Decide fair orders for your group. Who buys coffee? Who picks first?

setup.shuffleHint: Shuffles the outcome positions once more so no one can predict the result in advance

howTo.heading: How to play
howTo.whatIsBody: The Ladder Game is a classic method for fairly deciding outcomes: who buys coffee, who picks first, who does the chore. You write participant names at the top and outcomes at the bottom, then follow a random ladder of vertical lines and horizontal rungs to determine each person's result. It's simple, fair, and fun—whether you're drawing lots for a team project, deciding chore order, or just having a good laugh.
howTo.howToBody: Start by selecting the number of players. Then enter each player's name and the possible outcomes (e.g., names vs. prizes or chores). If you leave outcomes blank, the game fills them in automatically. Click 'Build ladder' to generate a random ladder. The ladder is built fairly using computer randomness, ensuring every player has an equal 1/N chance of any outcome. Click on a player's name to reveal their result by tracing the path, or use 'Reveal all' to show everyone at once. You can reshuffle for a new ladder or reset to change players and outcomes.
howTo.featuresBody: Turn on 'Shuffle results' and the outcome slots are randomized so no one can predict the result in advance. Raise the 'Tension' and the ladder draws more horizontal rungs for a denser, more suspenseful trace—without changing fairness or the final result at all. 'Auto-suggest names' fills the board with fruit emoji and names for a quick start, while sound and confetti add to the fun of each reveal. Download the finished result as an image to share straight to a group chat. You can also play entirely by keyboard—number keys reveal a player, A reveals everyone, and R reshuffles.
howTo.useCasesBody: Deciding who buys the coffee among friends, fairly splitting presentation or role order in a team project, drawing who gets a penalty at a gathering, or randomly assigning prizes or seats. Because the result is traced by a visible line, everyone can see it's fair.
howTo.tipsBody: Enter the players and outcomes, build the ladder, then tap a name to follow its path down. 'Shuffle outcomes' randomizes where each result sits to make it harder to predict, and raising the tension adds more rungs for extra suspense. You can save the result as an image to share.

useCases.heading: When the Ladder Game comes in handy
useCases.lead: From a simple bet to an awkward turn order, one round of the ladder settles it cleanly—no arguments afterward.

items.title: Coffee & lunch runs
items.body: Deciding who buys the coffee or covers lunch today. It's fairer than rock-paper-scissors, and because everyone watches the result unfold, no one grumbles about it later.

items.title: Chores & duties
items.body: Splitting up the dishes, recycling, or note-taking—the jobs no one volunteers for. Just enter the names and the duties, and it pairs them up at random.

items.title: Presentation & game order
items.body: Speaking order, karaoke turns, who goes first in a board game. When you need a full 1st-to-last ranking, switch to 'Ranking' mode to sort it all at once.

items.title: Making teams or groups
items.body: Picking sides or forming groups. Put the team names in the outcome slots and players get assigned to teams automatically.

items.title: Gift & prize exchanges
items.body: Secret Santa, ladder gifts, prize draws—any time you need to decide who gets what. Keep the outcomes hidden and reveal them one at a time to double the suspense.

items.q: Is the ladder game really fair?
items.a: Yes, absolutely. This digital ladder uses proven fair randomness—every player gets exactly 1/N probability of any outcome, regardless of starting position. Hand-drawn ladders often favor the center (a phenomenon called the 'ugly truth' in Korean), but this version eliminates that bias entirely.

items.q: How many players can play?
items.a: You can play with 2 to 10 players.

items.q: Does starting position matter?
items.a: No. Your result is completely independent of where you start. Enabling 'Hide results' before building the ladder makes it even more fair and prevents anyone from gaming the system.

items.q: Can I reshuffle the ladder?
items.a: Yes. Once all results are revealed, click 'Reshuffle' to generate a brand new ladder while keeping all the player names and outcomes.

items.q: Can I share the results?
items.a: Of course. Use the 'Copy results' button to copy the outcome mapping as text, then paste it into a message or email to share with your group.

items.q: Does it work on mobile?
items.a: Yes. The Ladder Game runs right in your mobile browser with nothing to install. If you have many players and the board gets wide, just scroll it left and right to see the whole ladder.

items.q: Are the names I type saved anywhere?
items.a: No. The player names and outcomes you enter are used only inside your browser and are never sent or stored anywhere. Refreshing the page clears them all, so you can use it without any privacy concerns.

items.q: Can I turn off the sound or animation?
items.a: Yes. Use 'Sound off' on the results panel to mute the effects. And if you enable 'reduce motion' in your device settings, the ladder-tracing animation and confetti are automatically minimized.

items.q: Can I share the result as an image?
items.a: Yes. Tap 'Download result' to save an image file showing every player and their outcome at a glance. Post it straight to a group chat or social media to share.

intro.title: Q&A a Day
intro.lead: Spend a year answering the same question every day. On the same date each year, you'll see your past answer alongside your new one. Every entry stays private in your browser—a personal, safe space to reflect.

benefits.heading: What one line a day leaves behind

items.title: You can see yourself grow
items.body: Answer the same question a year later and changes you never noticed come into sharp focus. Seeing last year's you and today's you side by side is more honest than any self-help book.

items.title: Consistency without pressure
items.body: One question a day, a sentence or two—that's plenty. No need to write long, no need to never miss a day. Because it's a small habit, it lasts; and because it lasts, it becomes a record you'll treasure.

items.title: A space that's entirely yours
items.body: Every answer is stored only in this browser and uploaded nowhere. With no worry about who might see it, you can write down even the things you could never say out loud.

examples.heading: The kind of questions you'll meet
examples.lead: The 365 questions start light and reach all the way to what matters most. A few of the prompts waiting on the calendar:
items: What is your purpose in life?
What are you reading right now?
Who do you want to know better?
Is there any present you'd like from Santa Claus?
examples.note: The question changes automatically each day based on the date.

howTo.heading: How to use Q&A a Day
howTo.whatBody: Q&A a Day is a year-long self-reflection journal. Every calendar date comes with one fixed, thoughtful question. You answer it once today, then next year on the same date, you'll see your old answer right alongside your new one. This creates a powerful 'then vs. now' mirror: you see exactly how you've changed, what you've learned, and what matters to you. All entries stay private in your browser.
howTo.howBody: The 'Today' tab shows today's question front and center with a text area to write your answer. It auto-saves as you type. Switch to the 'Calendar' tab to browse any date and edit past answers—or even write ahead for future dates. The 'Journal' tab lets you search your entire history and filter by year. Use the 'Settings' tab to back up your entries (download a JSON file), restore from a backup, or reset everything. That's it—simple and private.
howTo.whyTitle: Why answer the same question daily?
howTo.whyBody: Repeating the same question year after year teaches you about yourself at a deeper level. Your answer evolves: your priorities shift, your worldview grows, your understanding deepens. When you look back at what you wrote a year ago, you see your own progress made tangible. The habit builds slowly—one answer a day—but after a year, you've created a precious record of who you are and how you're becoming.
howTo.useCasesBody: When you want to briefly look back on your day as it winds down, when you want to compare who you are on the same day each year (a New Year, a birthday), when you want a steady record of your thoughts for a reflection or a personal statement, or when you want to build a mind-settling habit like a gratitude journal.
howTo.tipsBody: There's no right answer, so what matters is leaving something every day, even a short line. Answers are stored only in this browser, so they disappear if you switch devices or clear your browser data—export a backup regularly from the Settings tab. Writing while looking at last year's answer for the same day makes the change easier to see.

items.q: Where are my answers stored?
items.a: Entirely in this browser's local storage. Your answers never leave your device and are never uploaded anywhere. Because of this, if you clear your browser data or switch devices, you'll lose everything unless you export a backup first. We strongly recommend regular backups.

items.q: Can I move my entries to another device?
items.a: Yes, via export and import. On your current device, use 'Settings' → 'Export' to download a JSON backup file. On your new device, open Q&A a Day, go to 'Settings' → 'Import', and select the file. Choose to merge (combine with any existing entries) or replace (start fresh with the imported entries).

items.q: Can I see what I wrote a year ago today?
items.a: Yes—that's the whole point. On the 'Today' tab, scroll down and you'll see a 'Past years' section showing all your previous answers for this calendar date, newest first.

items.q: What if I want to skip a day?
items.a: No problem. You don't have to answer every day. Come back whenever you want. You can also write ahead for future dates if you're feeling inspired.

items.q: What happens on Leap Day (February 29)?
items.a: The dataset has exactly 365 questions for 365 days. On February 29 (in leap years), you'll see the February 28 question—the same one non-leap years use.

items.q: Can my entries disappear?
items.a: Yes, if you clear your browser's cached data or storage. Since entries are only stored locally, they're vulnerable to the usual browser actions. That's why consistent backups are essential. With a backup file, you can always restore everything.

items.q: Is this journal shareable?
items.a: Your entries are private by default. No one can see them unless you intentionally share the export file. It's a personal space, just for you.

items.q: Does it work offline?
items.a: Once the page has loaded, writing and saving all happen inside your browser, so it keeps working even if your internet connection drops. Every answer is stored only on this device.

items.q: If I switch languages, do the questions change too?
items.a: Yes. There's a separate set of questions in Korean and English, so switching languages shows the same date's question in that language. Answers you've already written are kept intact.

From MZ slang to cutting-edge tech terminology, explore language that's constantly evolving, with definitions in both Korean and English. Use the search box to find a specific term or browse by category. Star your favorite terms to save them for quick access, and your recently viewed terms are automatically saved so you can easily find them again.
howTo.useCasesBody: When you're curious about a word you're seeing for the first time online or on social media, when you want to understand a slang term or meme that keeps popping up in the news or a community, when checking an unfamiliar expression in a cross-generation conversation, or when you want to pick up new tech and trend terms in both Korean and English.

The category tabs let you gather just the area you care about (MZ slang, tech terms, and so on), and starring a term makes it easy to find again next time. items.q: What are new words / slang?

items.q: What kinds of terms are included?

items.q: Where are my favorite terms saved?

items.q: Can I see Korean and English definitions together?

items.q: Can I add new terms?

whatIs.title: What is URL encoding?
whatIs.body: URL encoding converts special characters into percent-encoded sequences starting with '%'. For example, a space becomes %20, and & becomes %26. This makes it safe to pass text in URLs.

steps.title: How to use it
steps.body: 1. Choose the encode or decode direction. 2. Type text and the result appears instantly. 3. Switch between Component / Full URI mode and the UTF-8 / EUC-KR charset as needed. 4. You can also edit key=value pairs directly in the query-parameter table. Copy the result to use it right away.

componentVsUri.title: Component vs Full URI
componentVsUri.body: Component mode is used for encoding query parameters or hash fragments—it encodes reserved characters like /, :, and ?. Full URI mode is for complete URLs—it preserves the URL structure characters (/, :, ?) so the URL remains valid.

utf8VsEuckr.title: UTF-8 vs EUC-KR
utf8VsEuckr.body: Most modern websites and systems use UTF-8. However, older Korean sites and legacy systems may use EUC-KR (CP949). For example, %C7%D1 is an invalid UTF-8 sequence, but when decoded as EUC-KR, it becomes the Korean character '한'.

useCases.title: When to use it
useCases.body: Safely encoding a link when it contains Korean, spaces, or special characters that would otherwise break it, decoding a URL full of '%' from somewhere else back into readable text, checking or editing query parameters like utm_source, or making sense of an address from an older Korean site that was encoded in EUC-KR.

tips.title: Tips and gotchas
tips.body: Use Component mode when you're only handling the query value after the question mark, and Full URI mode for a complete address. If the result shows garbled Korean, switch the charset (UTF-8 ↔ EUC-KR). Encoding and decoding are not encryption, so don't rely on them to protect sensitive information.

items.q: Why is URL encoding necessary?
items.a: Certain characters in URLs (space, &, %, etc.) have special meaning. Encoding them makes it safe to transmit data within URLs.

items.q: What's the difference between Component and Full URI mode?
items.a: Component mode encodes the values in query strings like ?name=hello&age=30. Full URI mode encodes a complete URL like https://example.com/path?a=1 while preserving the URL structure (/, :, ?).

items.q: What is EUC-KR?
items.a: EUC-KR (Extended Unix Code for Korean) is a character encoding for Korean text, also known as CP949. It was widely used by Korean websites in the early 2000s.

items.q: What happens if I encode already-encoded text?
items.a: It gets double-encoded. For example, %20 becomes %2520. This tool warns you if it detects %xx patterns.

items.q: What does the Query String Editor do?
items.a: It parses query strings like ?name=Alice&age=30 into a table. Edit the values and click 'Rebuild query string' to get the encoded result.

items.q: Where is my input data stored?
items.a: All processing happens locally in your browser. Nothing is sent over the network or stored on a server. Only the 10 most recent inputs are saved in localStorage, and you can clear them anytime.

intro.title: Cron Expression Parser
intro.lead: Convert cryptic Unix cron expressions to clear, human-readable descriptions and compute upcoming run times in your timezone.

items.title: What is this tool?
items.body: A cron expression parser turns an expression like '0 9 * * 1-5' into a human-readable description and calculates the next run times. It supports both Unix/Linux crontab (5 fields) and Java Quartz (6–7 fields), and breaks down what each field means. All parsing runs inside your own browser.

items.title: Understanding Fields
items.body: A cron expression has 5 fields: minute (0–59), hour (0–23), day-of-month (1–31), month (1–12), day-of-week (0–6). Each field defines when the job runs.

items.title: Wildcards & Ranges
items.body: The * (wildcard) means "any value". Ranges (1-5) mean 1,2,3,4,5, and steps (*/15) mean every 15th value (e.g., 0,15,30,45 for minutes).

items.title: Common Patterns
items.body: Weekdays at 9 AM is 0 9 * * MON-FRI, first of month is 0 0 1 * *, and hourly is 0 * * * *.

items.title: Timezone Awareness
items.body: This tool calculates upcoming run times in your selected timezone, and daylight saving time (DST) transitions are handled automatically.

items.title: When to use it
items.body: Checking that an expression does what you intend before adding a job to a server's crontab, making sense of someone else's cron config, validating a Quartz scheduler expression for something like Spring, or previewing exactly when the next run will be.

items.title: Tips
items.body: Both 0 and 7 mean Sunday for day-of-week, and you can use names (MON–FRI). If you set both a day-of-month and a day-of-week, the job runs when either one matches (OR), so take care. Quartz format puts a seconds field first and supports extensions like L (last day), W (nearest weekday), and # (nth weekday).

items.q: What's the difference between Unix cron and Quartz format?
items.a: Unix/Linux crontab uses 5 fields (minute hour day month weekday). Java Quartz adds a seconds field at the front for 6–7 fields and supports extensions like L (last day), W (nearest weekday), # (nth weekday), and ?. Use the mode toggle at the top to switch between the two.

items.q: My expression isn't running as expected.
items.a: First check that the description and "next run times" match what you intended. Common causes are specifying both a day-of-month and a day-of-week (which run with OR logic), confusing the weekday number (0 = Sunday), and timezone differences. The field breakdown table shows how each field was interpreted.

items.q: What happens when both day-of-month and day-of-week are specified?
items.a: According to standard cron semantics, if both are specified, OR logic applies: the job runs on the specified day-of-month OR the specified day-of-week. E.g., 0 9 15 * MON runs on the 15th of any month OR any Monday at 9 AM.

items.q: Why is day-of-week 0–6, with 0 = Sunday?
items.a: This is the standard POSIX cron convention: 0 = Sunday, 1 = Monday, …, 6 = Saturday. Some systems allow 7 for Sunday; this tool converts 7 to 0.

items.q: What happens with impossible dates like February 29 or April 31?
items.a: February 29 exists only in leap years, so a job on Feb 29 runs only in leap years. April 31 doesn't exist, so a job on that date is skipped.

items.q: What happens during daylight saving time (DST) transitions?
items.a: During spring DST transition, skipped times are skipped. During fall transition, times that occur twice may run twice. This tool uses your browser's timezone database to handle DST automatically.

howTo.heading: How to Use Bookmarks
howTo.whatIsBody: Curated Bookmarks brings together trusted resources across architecture, frontend, design, development, and more. Unlike crowdsourced collections, every resource is hand-picked and verified by editors to ensure consistent quality and reliability. Resources are organized by topic so you can quickly find great materials in your field of interest.
howTo.howToBody: Select a topic from the filter or search for keywords. Click on a topic card to see the curated links in that area. Star your favorites to save them, and your recently viewed topics are automatically tracked for quick access.
howTo.featuresBody: Filter by topic, search in real-time, save favorites, track recently viewed topics. Every link is hand-curated by editors for quality and relevance, so you get trusted resources without having to sift through noise.
howTo.useCasesBody: Finding vetted resources on a specific topic (the eGovFrame standard framework, prompt engineering, and so on) in one place, needing a good set of starting links as you begin studying or working, or browsing useful sites gathered by topic.
howTo.tipsBody: Open a topic tab to see its link collection, and use search to narrow to what you want. Star topics you visit often to find them in the Favorites tab, and recently viewed items are saved automatically. Each link opens safely in a new tab.

items.q: Can I add new topics?
items.a: We currently feature editor-curated topics. You can submit new topic suggestions via the Contact page.

items.q: How are links selected?
items.a: Editors personally visit and verify each resource to ensure it meets our standards for expertise and trustworthiness. We regularly check links to maintain their validity.

items.q: Where are my favorites and recent history stored?
items.a: Everything is stored only in your browser and never uploaded or sent anywhere. Refresh the page and your saved favorites and recent history will still be there.

items.q: Do links open in a new tab?
items.a: Yes, all external links open in a new tab so you stay on this page while browsing multiple resources.

items.q: What if a link doesn't work?
items.a: Editors check links regularly, but external sites can change. If you find a broken link, please let us know via the Contact page.

howTo.whatIsBody: Editor-curated Top N lists across a variety of fields. From the latest AI models to programming languages, movies, and music—all from trusted sources.
howTo.howToBody: Select a field from the filter or search for keywords. Click a ranking card to see detailed items and sources. Star your favorites to save them.
howTo.featuresBody: Filter by field, search in real-time, save favorites, track recently viewed. Every ranking shows its source and date for transparency.
howTo.useCasesBody: Quickly scanning the latest rankings in an area you care about (programming language popularity, LLM leaderboards, wealth rankings, and so on), finding a trustworthy ranking with a clear source and as-of date, or reading the trend against previous snapshots.
howTo.tipsBody: Each ranking shows its source and as-of date in a banner, so check how it was scored before reading the numbers. Item descriptions include the change versus the previous snapshot, making the trend easy to read. Tables are ordered by rank and score.

items.q: Are rankings updated in real-time?

items.q: Can I vote on rankings?

howTo.heading: How to use Age Calculator
howTo.whatIsBody: Age Calculator helps you understand the three ways of calculating age used in Korea: legal age (만 나이), calendar-year age, and traditional counting age. Since Korea unified to legal age in 2023, most official contexts use that standard—but many people still think in calendar-year terms or refer back to traditional counting. This tool clears up the confusion by showing all three at once, plus your zodiac sign and other birth details.
howTo.howToBody: Enter your birthdate in YYYY-MM-DD format (for example, 2000-03-15) in the birthdate field. By default, the tool calculates age as of today. If you want to see what your age was on a different date, turn on the 'Reference date' toggle and pick a specific date. Press Enter or finish typing to see your results instantly.
howTo.featuresBody: Save your family members' and friends' birthdates so you can instantly recalculate by clicking their name. The 'Recent calculations' list automatically saves dates you've looked up before, so you don't have to type them again. Copy your results and paste them into messages or notes to share with others.
howTo.useCasesBody: When you need the exact age to put on a form after Korea unified on 'international age', when calculating the age gap with a friend or celebrity, when checking your age as of a specific date (like enrollment or retirement), or when you're curious how many days you've lived or how many remain until your next birthday.
howTo.tipsBody: Enter a birthdate to compare international age, year age, and Korean age at a glance. Enter a lunar birthday and it calculates this year's solar birthday and the days until the next one on the lunar calendar. Birthdays you check often are saved in 'Recent calculations' to reload with one click.

items.q: What's the difference between legal age, calendar year age, and traditional age?
items.a: Legal age is how many times your birthday has passed since birth—it's the international standard and Korea's official legal standard. Calendar-year age is simply the current year minus your birth year. Traditional age is a Korean custom where you're considered 1 year old at birth and gain a year on January 1st every year. Korea officially switched to legal age in June 2023.

items.q: What if I was born on February 29 (leap day)?
items.a: People born on February 29 celebrate their birthday on February 28 or March 1 in non-leap years. This tool automatically calculates your age correctly regardless of the reference year.

items.q: Is my information saved?
items.a: Your recent calculation history and saved people are stored only in this browser's local storage and never sent to any server. If you clear your browser data, this information will be deleted.

items.q: Can I calculate age as of a past or future date?
items.a: Yes, turn on the 'Reference date' toggle to pick any date in the past or future. This lets you calculate what someone's age was or will be on a specific date.

items.q: Does it work on mobile?
items.a: Yes, it works great on mobile browsers with no app needed.

items.q: Are the zodiac and star sign calculations accurate?
items.a: This tool uses traditional lunar-based calculation methods. The zodiac and star sign follow the conventions commonly used in Korean culture. (More precise lunar calendar calculations may be added in future updates)

howTo.heading: How to Use the Lunar-Solar Calendar Converter
howTo.whatIsTitle: What's the difference between lunar and solar calendars?
howTo.whatIsBody: The lunar calendar (陰曆) is based on the moon's phases and is used for traditional Korean festivals like Lunar New Year (설날) and Chuseok (추석). The solar (Gregorian) calendar is based on Earth's orbit around the sun and is the international standard. The same date has different notations in each system—this tool makes conversion between them simple.
howTo.sexagenaryTitle: What are the sexagenary cycle and zodiac animals?
howTo.sexagenaryBody: The sexagenary cycle (干支, Gapja) combines 10 heavenly stems and 12 earthly branches, repeating every 60 years. For example, 2024 is '갑진년' (Wood Dragon), and 2023 is '계묘년' (Water Rabbit). The 12 zodiac animals represent the 12 earthly branches: Rat (자), Ox (축), Tiger (인), Rabbit (묘), Dragon (진), Snake (사), Horse (오), Goat (미), Monkey (신), Rooster (유), Dog (술), Pig (해). Used for age reckoning and fortune-telling in Korea, China, Japan, and beyond.
howTo.howToBody: Enter a solar (Gregorian) date in the 'Solar' tab, and the tool shows the equivalent lunar date, sexagenary, and zodiac. Or enter a lunar date in the 'Lunar' tab to convert to solar. If the year has a leap month, toggle the 'Leap Month' checkbox for accurate conversion. Click 'Check Today' to instantly see the lunar date and sexagenary for the current date.
howTo.useCasesBody: Checking the solar date of a birthday or ancestral rite kept on the lunar calendar, finding what solar date a holiday like Seollal or Chuseok falls on this year, looking up the sexagenary year (like Gapjin) used in Korean fortune-telling, or moving a lunar date from an old document onto today's calendar.
howTo.tipsBody: Enter the date you know (solar or lunar) and it instantly converts to the other. In years with a leap month, you can select whether it's the leap month, so match a rite or birthday exactly. The result also shows the sexagenary cycle (heavenly stem and earthly branch) and the zodiac animal. The supported range is 1391–2050 (per KASI).

items.q: What is a leap month (윤달) in the lunar calendar?
items.a: The lunar calendar is shorter than the solar year by about 11 days. To keep it aligned with the seasons, a 'leap month' (윤달) is inserted approximately 7 times every 19 years instead of a regular month. For example, 2023 had a leap second month (윤2월). The position of the leap month varies by year—use this tool to find it.

items.q: How can I quickly find Lunar New Year and Chuseok dates?
items.a: Enter the year in the Solar tab and try lunar month 1, day 1 for Lunar New Year (설날), and lunar month 8, day 15 for Chuseok (추석). The tool will convert them to solar dates. For example, Lunar New Year 2024 (음력 1월 1일) = February 10, 2024.

items.q: What is the date range for this tool?
items.a: This converter supports dates from 1901 to 2050. Dates outside this range cannot be calculated.

items.q: Is the sexagenary calculation accurate?
items.a: Yes, the sexagenary cycle is calculated from a fixed formula: (year - 4) % 60, so it is precise. However, some traditions use the winter solstice (冬至) as the reference point, so different schools or practices may have slightly different conventions.

items.q: Where are recent conversions stored?
items.a: Recent conversions are stored only in your browser's local storage and are never sent to any server. If you clear your browser data, the history will be deleted.

items.q: Does it work on mobile?
items.a: Yes, the converter works perfectly on mobile browsers with no app installation needed.

intro.title: QR Code Generator
intro.lead: Convert text, URLs, Wi-Fi credentials, and contact info into QR codes. Customize colors and download as PNG or SVG.

howTo.title: How to generate a QR code
howTo.whatIsBody: A QR code generator turns text, a URL, Wi-Fi, contact details (vCard), an email, or an SMS into a scannable QR code. You can adjust the foreground and background colors, size, quiet zone (margin), and error-correction level, add a logo in the center, and download as PNG or SVG or copy to the clipboard. Every code is generated inside your own browser.
howTo.useCasesBody: Putting a Wi-Fi-join QR code on a cafe or restaurant table, adding contact details or a website link to a business card or poster, linking an event notice or menu with a single code, or generally bridging printed material to something online.
howTo.tipsBody: For reliable scanning, keep enough contrast between the foreground and background (a light background with a dark code works well). When adding a logo or printing, a higher error-correction level (Q or H) keeps it readable even if part is covered. SVG is better for print and enlargement since it doesn't pixelate. Shrinking the quiet zone (margin) too much can hurt scan reliability.
howTo.content:
1. Select a mode — Choose from the tabs at the top: Text, URL, Wi-Fi, vCard, Email, or SMS.

2. Enter your data — Depending on the mode, enter the relevant information. For text and URLs, just type. For Wi-Fi, enter the network name and password. For vCard, add your name and contact details.

3. Adjust the style — (Optional) Customize the foreground color (dark) and background color (light). You can also adjust the size and quiet zone (white margin). Make sure there's enough contrast between colors for easy scanning.

4. Choose an error correction level — Pick from L (7%), M (15%), Q (25%), or H (30%). Higher levels can recover from more damage but make the code larger.

5. Add a logo — (Optional) Upload an image to place in the center of the QR code. Use a simple logo to avoid interfering with scanners.

6. Download or copy — Save the QR code as PNG or SVG, or copy it to your clipboard to paste directly elsewhere.

items.q: What is a QR code?
items.a: A QR code (Quick Response Code) is a 2D barcode that stores information. Scan it with a smartphone camera or QR reader to quickly retrieve the encoded data. This tool lets you create QR codes from text, URLs, Wi-Fi info, contact details, and more.

items.q: Do QR codes work with custom colors?
items.a: Yes, they work with any colors as long as there's enough contrast between the foreground (dark) and background (light). This tool warns you if contrast is too low to be easily scanned. Aim for a contrast value of 50 or higher.

items.q: Where is my generated QR code stored?
items.a: Your QR code is generated only in your browser—nowhere else. It exists only on this page until you download or copy it. No data is sent to any server, so you can use this tool with complete privacy.

items.q: What's the difference between PNG and SVG?
items.a: PNG is a raster image—small file size, but may look blurry if enlarged. SVG is a vector image—larger file size, but infinitely scalable without loss of quality. Use SVG for printing or large displays, PNG for web and messaging.

items.q: How do I use a Wi-Fi QR code?
items.a: Scan the QR code with your phone's camera or a QR reader app. Your device will automatically detect the Wi-Fi network details and let you connect with one tap. Great for sharing Wi-Fi without saying the password out loud.

items.q: What does error correction level do?
items.a: It lets QR codes tolerate damage. L recovers from ~7% damage, M from ~15%, Q from ~25%, and H from ~30%. Higher levels create more complex codes, so they're larger for the same data. Choose higher levels if your QR code might get dirty or damaged.

items.q: What's the maximum amount of data I can put in a QR code?
items.a: Text can hold up to ~2953 characters depending on the error correction level. URLs and Wi-Fi info have shorter limits. The tool will tell you if your input is too long.

items.q: Can I put a logo in the middle of a QR code?
items.a: Yes, but keep it simple. The logo can cover about 25% of the center area without breaking the code, but complex logos might prevent scanners from reading it. A simple, small logo works best.

howTo.title: Transparent background guide
howTo.whatIsBody: This tool erases a solid (single-color) background and turns it into a transparent PNG. It detects the background color automatically, or you can pick it yourself with the eyedropper, and then makes pixels close to that color transparent. You can adjust the tolerance and the edge softness (feathering), and choose between erasing only the area connected to the background or erasing every pixel of the same color. Images are never uploaded to a server — everything is processed inside your own browser.
howTo.s1Body: Upload an image with a solid background in PNG, JPEG, or WebP format.
howTo.s2Body: Click Auto-Detect to sample from image corners, or use the color picker to manually select.
howTo.s3Body: Move the slider to control how similar colors are removed. Lower = precise, Higher = forgiving.
howTo.s4Body: Set edge smoothness from 0–20 pixels. Higher values create a softer anti-alias effect.
howTo.s5Body: Choose between Flood-Fill (connected areas only) or All Matching (every matching pixel).
howTo.s6Body: Download as transparent PNG or copy directly to clipboard.
howTo.whenToUse: Great for tidying a white-background product photo to drop onto a store page or document, making a logo or a scanned stamp or signature on a solid background transparent, or clearing a solid area out of a screenshot. For photos with busy backgrounds or person cutouts, color alone can't separate the subject, so an AI-based background remover works better.
howTo.tipsBody: If auto-detect doesn't catch the background exactly, sample the background color yourself with the eyedropper. If a colored fringe is left behind, raise the tolerance a little; if the subject starts disappearing, lower it. When edges look hard, a feathering of about 2–5 pixels makes them look natural. Any areas that were already transparent in the original are preserved.

items.q: Is my image uploaded?
items.a: No. All processing happens locally in your browser. Your image is never uploaded anywhere.

items.q: How large is the output PNG?
items.a: It depends on the original size. Downscaled images (over 4096px) will be significantly smaller.

items.q: What's the difference between Flood-Fill and All Matching modes?
items.a: Flood-Fill: removes only colors connected to the background (preserves matching colors inside objects). All Matching: removes every pixel matching the background color.

items.q: What does Feather do?
items.a: Feather smooths the edges. Higher values create a softer, anti-aliased transition between transparent and opaque areas.

items.q: What if my image already has transparency?
items.a: Existing transparency is preserved. The new removal effect multiplies with it.

items.q: Is there a maximum image size?
items.a: No limit. Images over 4096px are automatically scaled for performance. Quality is preserved for the scaled version.

howTo.useCasesBody: As a party game where teams compete at a dinner, gathering, or trip, as a classroom recreation or a workshop ice-breaker, or as something the whole family can enjoy across generations. Since only the host looks at the screen, it works great projected onto a big screen for a group.
howTo.tipsBody: Have the host watch the screen alone and ask the guessers to look away. Using Space (correct) and the arrows (pass) keeps the rhythm fast. Raising the difficulty and round time adds tension, and turning on 'shuffle words' changes the order every time even for the same deck.

items.q: What is Speed Quiz?
items.a: Speed Quiz is a presenter-led party game where one person displays prompt words on a big screen one at a time, others shout answers, and the presenter records correct guesses, passes, or timeouts. It's great for classrooms, parties, and team events.

items.q: How many people can play?
items.a: There's no limit! You can play with 2 people or 100+. One person acts as the presenter (controlling the screen) and everyone else participates.

items.q: Can I control it with just a keyboard?
items.a: Yes, absolutely. Use Space for the next word, Right arrow to pass, Left arrow to undo, and Esc to end. This makes it easy to run the game fast without a mouse.

items.q: How do hints work?
items.a: Before starting, turn on 'Show hints' in the setup. During the game, each word will display an optional hint below it. Disable hints if you want a harder challenge.

items.q: Where is my data stored?
items.a: All game data stays in your browser's local storage—nothing is sent to any server. Game progress resets on page refresh, but your favorites and recent games are saved locally.

cheatsheet.fullMatch: The whole matched string in replacement

error.timeout: This regex is too slow (possible catastrophic backtracking)

howTo.title: How to Use Find & Replace
howTo.whatIsBody: Find & Replace transforms text by applying several 'find → replace' rules at once. Rules apply top to bottom in order, and each rule can turn on regex, case sensitivity, whole-word, or first-only options. It's not an AI that rewrites your sentences — it precisely swaps the words and phrases you define — and everything runs inside your own browser.
howTo.useCasesBody: Renaming several names or terms across a whole document at once, cleaning up special characters or quotes in pasted text, using regex to change a repeating pattern in logs or data, or standardizing wording during translation or proofreading.
steps: 1. Paste the text you want to transform above.
2. Add one or more find → replace rules.
4. Copy the result or download it as a .txt file.

tips.title: Stack multiple rules
tips.body: Rules apply top to bottom, and each rule sees the output of the one above it, so ordering is a feature. For example, replace "cat" with "tiger", then "tiger" with "lion", and every original "cat" ends up as "lion".

tips.title: Regex is optional
tips.body: By default, "find" matches literal text and needs no regex knowledge. Toggle "Regex" per rule to treat the pattern as a JavaScript regular expression with capture groups (2026)-(07) and references like $1-$2.

tips.title: Precision options
tips.body: Turn on "Case-sensitive" to distinguish Hello from hello. Turn on "Whole word" so "test" is skipped inside "testing".

items.q: Does a bad regex freeze my tab?
items.a: The MVP uses a best-effort deadline guard (500ms timeout). Even a catastrophic-backtracking regex won't freeze your tab permanently, but you may see a brief delay. Phase 2 will upgrade to a Web Worker for a hard guarantee.

items.q: Where are my saved rule-sets stored?
items.a: Your rule-sets are stored in your browser's localStorage. Nothing is sent to a server. Clearing your browser cache will delete them.

items.q: Can I replace one at a time using "First only"?
items.a: Yes, toggle "First only" to replace only the first match. For step-by-step replacement, you'd need to reload and repeat—this tool is optimized for replace-all.

items.q: Does it change my original text?
items.a: No. Your original stays as-is and the transformed result is shown separately. Copy the result or download it as a .txt to use it.

items.q: Does it work offline?
items.a: Yes. Every replacement runs inside your browser, so it works offline, and the text you enter is never sent anywhere.

intro.title: Character & Word Counter
intro.lead: Count characters, words, sentences in real-time.

items.q: Why do character counts with and without spaces differ?
items.a: Spaces include tabs, line breaks, etc. Most apps (social media, forms) set limits by character count including spaces.

items.q: How are emoji counted?
items.a: Emoji and ZWJ sequences count as 1 character each (via Intl.Segmenter).

items.q: How is reading time calculated?
items.a: We divide word count by your reading speed (default 200 WPM).

items.q: How is speaking time calculated?
items.a: We divide word count by your speaking speed (default 130 WPM).

items.q: Why do bytes differ from character count?
items.a: In UTF-8, Korean characters and emoji take multiple bytes. Byte limits are common in programming.

items.q: How do I check a character limit?
items.a: Pick a preset like Twitter or SMS, or type your own number, and the remaining count and any overage are shown in color (green → orange → red).

items.q: Does it work offline?
items.a: Yes. Every calculation runs inside your browser, so it works offline, and the text you enter is never sent anywhere.

howTo.title: Character & word counter guide
howTo.whatIsBody: A character and word counter tallies the characters (with and without spaces), words, sentences, paragraphs, lines, and bytes (UTF-8) of text you type or paste, live as you go. It also estimates reading and speaking time, and counts characters made of several code points — like emoji or composed Korean syllables — as one. You can pick a character-limit preset like Twitter (280) or SMS and watch the remaining count change color, and every calculation runs inside your own browser.

steps.text: Paste or type text into the input field.
All metrics update in real-time as you type.
Optionally select a character limit (Twitter, Meta, custom).
Copy the text or metrics for use elsewhere.

howTo.useCasesBody: Fitting a character limit on social media like Twitter or Instagram, checking the length of a cover letter or report (characters excluding spaces), estimating how long a script will take to read aloud, or seeing how many SMS segments a message splits into by byte count. It's also commonly used to price translation or proofreading work by length.
howTo.tipsBody: "With spaces" and "without spaces" counts are required in different situations, so check which the form wants. In UTF-8 a Korean character is 3 bytes, so byte-based limits are reached sooner than the character count suggests. Characters built from several code points — emoji, family emoji — are still counted as one, the way a person sees them. The preset's color (green → orange → red) shows at a glance how close you are to the limit.

howTo.title: How to Use the Roulette
howTo.whatIsBody: The decision roulette picks one option at random from a list you write. You can give each option a different weight to adjust its odds, remove the winner and spin again, enjoy sound and confetti effects, and save and load sets. Draws use a cryptographically secure random number so they're fair, and everything runs inside your own browser.
howTo.useCasesBody: Deciding lunch or which cafe to go to, fixing a fair order for presentations or chores, drawing who gets a penalty or a prize at a gathering, or quickly settling on one of several options. It also works for picking a random winner among many, like an event giveaway.
howTo.tipsBody: To make a particular option come up more often, raise its weight (they all start at 1). To keep drawing without repeats, turn on "remove winner." Save a list you use often as a set so you can load it again next time.
howTo.content:
**Step 1: Add Options**
Enter your options in the input field at the top. You can add between 2 and 30 options, such as "Lunch", "Cafe", "Movie", etc. Separate names with commas to add several at once ("Pizza, Pasta, Salad"), or paste a newline-separated list to add every line in one go.

**Step 2: Adjust Weights (Optional)**
You can adjust the weight of each option to change the probability of being selected. Options with higher weights are more likely to win. The default weight is 1 for all options.

**Step 3: Spin**
Click the "Spin Now!" button to make the wheel spin and reveal the winner.

**Step 4: View Result**
The selected option is displayed prominently, with optional sound and animation effects.

**Additional Features:**
- **Save Sets**: Save frequently used option combinations with custom names for quick reuse.
- **Remove Winner Mode**: Enable "Remove Winner Mode" to exclude previously selected options and continue spinning.
- **Volume Control**: Adjust the volume slider or mute the sound effects in the settings.

items.q: Is the roulette truly fair?
items.a: Yes, it's 100% fair. This tool uses cryptographic random number generation (crypto.getRandomValues) to ensure each option has an equal probability. If you set weights, the probability is determined by the exact ratio of the weights.

items.q: How many options can I add?
items.a: You can add a minimum of 2 and a maximum of 30 options. The more options you add, the denser the wheel becomes, but all options remain visible.

items.q: Can I add multiple options at once?
items.a: Yes. Type comma-separated names in the input ("Pizza, Pasta, Salad") to add them all at once. You can also paste a newline-separated list copied from a notes app — each line becomes an option. Duplicate names are skipped automatically.

items.q: How do weights work?
items.a: Each option's weight is proportional to its winning probability. For example, if option A has weight 1 and option B has weight 2, then B is twice as likely to be selected as A.

items.q: Where are my saved sets stored?
items.a: All sets are saved in your browser's local storage and are never sent anywhere. Your saved sets will remain even after refreshing the page.

items.q: What is Remove Winner Mode?
items.a: This mode allows you to exclude the previously selected option and spin again. It's useful when you need to make multiple sequential selections, such as determining the order for a group.

items.q: Why is there no sound?
items.a: Your browser or device may be muted. Check your browser's audio settings and the device's volume. Also, if your OS has "Reduce Motion" enabled, sounds may not play. Check the sound toggle in the Settings tab as well.

items.q: Can I edit options?
items.a: Yes, you can edit the option names in the input field next to each option. You can also adjust the weight. To remove an option entirely, click the delete button (×).

items.q: Does it work on mobile devices?
items.a: Yes, it works on all devices. The interface is touch-friendly and fully responsive, optimized for small screens.

items.title: Generate in 3 Steps
items.description: **Step 1:** Choose how many games to generate (1–10). **Step 2:** Optionally specify numbers to always include (up to 5) and numbers to exclude (up to 39). **Step 3:** Click "Generate" to create fair random draws using cryptographic randomness.

items.title: Cryptographically Secure Randomness
items.description: This tool uses `crypto.getRandomValues` with Fisher–Yates shuffle to ensure every eligible number has equal probability. No visual bias, no predictable patterns. Statistical validation (chi-square test) confirms fairness.

items.q: Can I win with these numbers?
items.a: Yes, but this tool doesn't improve your odds. All lottery combinations have equal probability. The generator produces truly random numbers only.

items.q: What's the difference between fixed and excluded?
items.a: **Fixed:** Numbers that will always appear in every game. For example, if you fix [7, 13], all generated games will include both numbers. **Excluded:** Numbers that will never be picked. For example, if you exclude [1, 2, 3], these numbers will never appear in any game.

items.q: Is this legal?
items.a: Yes, it's legal. This tool only generates random numbers and does not encourage gambling. Lottery play is your personal choice and responsibility.

items.q: Can this tool predict winning numbers?
items.a: No, absolutely not. This tool only generates truly random numbers. It does not analyze past winning numbers or predict future draws. Lottery drawings are completely independent each week; previous results have no influence on future ones.

items.q: Do my odds improve if I generate more numbers?
items.a: No. Whether you generate 1 game or 10 games, each game has the same odds of winning: approximately 1 in 8,145,060. Generating more numbers does not increase your chances. Lottery is entirely a game of chance.

items.q: Where are my generation records stored?
items.a: All generation records are stored only in your browser's localStorage. They are never sent to a server or stored remotely. Records will not appear on other devices or browsers. Clearing your browser cache will delete the history.

intro.lead: Convert text and files to Base64 and back. UTF-8 safe, standard and URL-safe variants, drag-drop file upload.

howTo.title: Base64 encoder & decoder guide
howTo.whatIsBody: Base64 is an encoding that represents binary data — like images or files — using only 64 printable ASCII characters. This tool converts text and files to Base64 and back, and supports both the standard variant (+, /) and the URL-safe variant (-, _). It handles emoji, Korean, and newlines without data loss via UTF-8, and every conversion runs inside your own browser.
howTo.howToBody: 1. Choose the encode or decode direction and the standard or URL-safe variant. 2. Type text or upload a file. 3. The result appears instantly, ready to copy or download as a file. When decoding, paste Base64 (or a data: URI) that encodes an image (PNG, JPEG, GIF, WebP, BMP, ICO, SVG) and the tool detects it automatically and shows a preview right away.
howTo.useCasesBody: Inlining small images, icons, fonts (woff2), or SVG as data: URIs in CSS or HTML to cut requests, inspecting Base64-wrapped data like an email attachment (MIME) or a certificate or key (PEM), carrying a binary such as a PDF inside JSON, XML, or a URL, or building an 'Authorization: Basic' header.
howTo.tipsBody: Base64 is not encryption — anyone can reverse it, so never use it to protect passwords or personal data. Encoding to Base64 makes data about 33% larger, so it's not suitable for big files. If the value goes into a URL or a file name, choose the URL-safe variant that uses - and _ instead of + and /.

items.q: Is Base64 encryption?
items.a: No. Base64 is encoding, not encryption. Anyone can decode Base64, so it provides no security. Do NOT use Base64 to protect sensitive information. Use proper encryption instead.

items.q: What's the difference between standard and URL-safe?
items.a: Standard Base64 uses + and / characters. URL-safe variant replaces these with - and _ to make it safe for URLs and filenames.

items.q: What's the file size limit?
items.a: This tool can handle files up to 5MB. For larger files, you may need to process them in multiple parts.

items.q: Is my data stored on a server?
items.a: No. All processing happens in your browser only. Nothing is sent to or stored on any server. Only your last used mode preference is stored locally.

items.q: Can I preview a decoded image on screen?
items.a: Yes. In the decode direction, paste the Base64 (or a data:image URL) of an image such as PNG, JPEG, GIF, WebP, BMP, ICO, or SVG and the tool recognizes it, renders a preview, and offers download and copy buttons. Non-image data is still decoded as text.

items.q: When is Base64 typically used?
items.a: Common uses include inlining small images, fonts, and SVG as data: URIs on the web; email attachments (MIME); storing certificates and keys (PEM) or Kubernetes Secrets; carrying binaries such as PDFs inside JSON, XML, or URLs; building 'Authorization: Basic' headers; and decoding JWT segments or unknown Base64 blobs to inspect their contents.

howTo.title: Unit converter guide
howTo.whatIsBody: A unit converter turns the same measurement into a different unit. It covers eight everyday categories — length, weight, temperature, area, volume, speed, digital storage, and time — and moves both ways between the metric system and imperial units (feet, pounds, Fahrenheit, and so on). Results are calculated the moment you type, and everything runs inside your own browser, so it works even without an internet connection.
howTo.howToBody: 1. Pick a category (length, weight, temperature, and so on) from the tabs. 2. Type a value and choose the from and to units. 3. The result updates live. Use the swap button to flip the two units, and the slider to set the decimal precision. Your most recent conversions are saved automatically so you can reuse them.
howTo.useCasesBody: Checking dimensions listed in inches or pounds against centimeters and kilograms when shopping overseas, converting a recipe's Fahrenheit temperature or cups and ounces to your own units, gauging miles and gallons as kilometers and liters while traveling, or turning feet into meters to lay out furniture — it gives a quick answer whenever units get mixed up. It's also handy for comparing file sizes in MB and GB, or checking how units relate for study.
howTo.tipsBody: Unlike other units, temperature is converted with an affine function (°C × 9/5 + 32 = °F) rather than a simple ratio, and this tool keeps round-trip conversions exact. If a result looks too long, lower the decimal slider; raise it when you need more precision. Every factor follows the International System of Units (SI), and most units other than temperature do not support negative values.

items.q: How is temperature converted?
items.a: Temperature uses an affine function rather than a simple ratio. Example: °C = (°F − 32) × 5/9 and °F = °C × 9/5 + 32, which keeps round-trip conversions exact.

items.q: Are the conversion factors accurate?
items.a: Every factor follows the International System of Units (SI). Only temperature uses an affine function; the rest convert by ratio against a base unit.

items.q: Can I convert negative values?
items.a: Most units don't support negative values. Temperature is the exception and can go below zero.

items.q: Does it work offline?
items.a: Yes. Every calculation runs inside your browser, so it works offline, and the values you enter are never sent anywhere.

items.q: Which units are supported?
items.a: Major metric and imperial units across eight categories: length, weight, temperature, area, volume, speed, digital storage, and time.

items.q: Why is the result rounded?
items.a: The screen shows values to the number of decimals you set with the slider. The internal calculation uses higher precision, so raise the slider to see a more exact value.

howTo.title: How to use My IP Address

whatIsIp.title: What is an IP address?
whatIsIp.body: An IP (Internet Protocol) address is a unique number that identifies your device on the internet. Like a home address, it ensures data reaches the correct destination online.

ipv4VsIpv6.title: What's the difference between IPv4 and IPv6?
ipv4VsIpv6.body: IPv4 is the older version supporting about 4.3 billion addresses, while IPv6 is the newer version supporting vastly more addresses. Most of the internet currently uses IPv4, but is gradually transitioning to IPv6.

publicVsPrivate.title: What's the difference between public and private IP?
publicVsPrivate.body: A public IP is an address identifiable across the entire internet—this is what this tool displays. A private IP is an address used only within a local network like your home or office.

dynamicVsStatic.title: What's the difference between dynamic and static IP?
dynamicVsStatic.body: A dynamic IP changes each time you connect to the internet, while a static IP always remains the same. Most home internet connections use dynamic IPs.

useCases.title: When to use it
useCases.body: When you need your public IP to set up port forwarding on a router for remote access or a game server, to confirm a VPN or proxy is actually on and your IP has changed, to register (whitelist) your IP with a service, or to troubleshoot a connection problem.

tips.title: Good to know
tips.body: What this tool shows is your public IP exposed to the internet, which differs from the private IP inside your home or office (like 192.168.x.x). A public IP reveals a rough region but not your exact home address. If you want to hide your real location, a VPN changes your IP to the VPN server's. This tool does not store your IP.

items.q: Is my IP address stored?
items.a: No. Jurepi does not store or log your IP address. It is fetched temporarily and then discarded.

items.q: Which service provides the IP lookup?
items.a: Jurepi uses two reliable IP lookup services: api.ipify.org and ipwho.is. If one fails, the tool automatically switches to the other.

items.q: Why is my IPv6 address not displayed?
items.a: Your network environment may not support IPv6. This is normal, and IPv6 adoption is ongoing globally.

items.q: What if my location is inaccurate?
items.a: The displayed location is an IP-based approximation. It may differ from GPS-based location and is provided by {provider}.

items.q: Is this tool safe?
items.a: Yes. All lookups happen in your browser only, and your IP is never sent to Jurepi servers. Since IP lookups are public data, there's no security risk.

intro.lead: Find your public IP address at a glance. See your IPv4, IPv6, and ISP information.

howTo.title: Knitting gauge guide
howTo.whatIsBody: Knitting gauge is the number of stitches and rows in a given measurement (usually 10cm / 4 inches) of knitted fabric. Once you know your finished size, gauge lets you calculate the stitches and rows to cast on, and if you have a pattern written for a different gauge, you can rescale it to your own. This calculator does all of that automatically.
howTo.howToBody: 1. Measure the stitches and rows within 10cm and enter them as your gauge. 2. Enter the width and length (cm) you want to make, and it calculates the stitches and rows you need. 3. To rescale a pattern, enter both the pattern's gauge and yours, and it gives you the adjusted counts.
howTo.useCasesBody: Calculating the stitch count to knit a sweater, hat, or scarf to the size you want, rescaling a pattern to your own gauge when a different yarn or needle changed it, or checking that the gauge you measured from a swatch matches the pattern.
howTo.tipsBody: For an accurate gauge, knit a generous swatch (say 15×15cm) with the exact yarn and needles you'll use, and measure the center 10cm excluding the edges. If your gauge is tighter than the pattern, go up a needle size; if looser, go down. Some yarns change size after washing and blocking, so washing the swatch once gives a more accurate result.

items.q: How do I measure my gauge?
items.a: Knit a small sample (swatch) about 10cm × 10cm (or 4in × 4in) using your yarn and needles. Count the number of stitches and rows within that area.

items.q: Why is gauge important?
items.a: A different gauge changes the finished size completely. The same stitch count with thinner yarn produces a smaller garment, while thicker yarn produces a larger one.

items.q: Can I mix cm and inches?
items.a: Yes, this calculator automatically converts between units. Use the toggle button to switch between cm and inches.

items.q: When do my saved gauges disappear?
items.a: Your gauges are stored in your browser. They'll be deleted if you clear your browser data or use private/incognito mode.

items.q: How do I rescale a pattern to my gauge?
items.a: Select the 'Pattern Rescale' tab. Enter the pattern's gauge (as written in the pattern) and stitch count. The calculator will show you the stitch count needed for your gauge.

intro.title: JSON Formatter & Validator
intro.lead: Quickly format, validate, and analyze JSON. Get precise error locations for invalid JSON.

howTo.title: JSON formatter & validator guide
howTo.whatIsBody: A JSON formatter and validator takes JSON that's crammed onto one line and indents it so it's readable, and when there's a syntax error it points to the exact line and column that's wrong. Beyond pretty-printing, it can minify (strip whitespace to shrink the size), sort keys, show the structure as a collapsible tree, highlight syntax, and even load JSON from a web address (URL). All processing happens inside your own browser.
howTo.howToBody: 1. Paste JSON into the input on the left, or load it from the URL of a public API or .json file. 2. Choose the indentation style and whether to sort keys. 3. The formatted result appears on the right, and any error is reported with its location. Copy the result or download it as a file, or minify it into a single line.
howTo.useCasesBody: Debugging an API response by expanding crammed JSON to see its structure, hunting down a typo or an unbalanced bracket in a config file (package.json, tsconfig, and the like), tidying JSON printed in a log, or sorting keys before sharing it with someone. It's also used to minify JSON down to reduce transfer size.
howTo.tipsBody: The error message gives a line and column — start there. The usual culprits are a missing comma, an unclosed quote or bracket, or a stray trailing comma after the last item. Loading from a URL needs the server to allow CORS; if it's blocked, just copy the content and paste it in. Sorting keys makes changes easier to compare (diff) in version control.

items.q: What is JSON?
items.a: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a text format for structured data. It's widely used in web APIs and configuration files.

items.q: Why format JSON?
items.a: Formatted JSON is easier to read, debug, and compare in version control.

items.q: Is my data private?
items.a: Yes, all processing happens in your browser. Your JSON never leaves your device.

items.q: Can I load from a URL?
items.a: Yes, paste the URL of a public JSON API or .json file. The server must allow CORS.

items.q: Does it accept comments or trailing commas?
items.a: It validates against standard JSON. Comments (//) and a comma after the last item aren't allowed in standard JSON and are flagged as errors, so stick to standard syntax.

items.q: How large a JSON can it handle?
items.a: It runs in your browser, so it depends on your device, but typical API responses and config files are handled with no trouble.

items.q: Where are my favorites saved?

howTo.title: What is JWT and Why Decode It?

items.q: What is JWT (JSON Web Token)?
items.a: JWT is an encoded JSON string designed to securely transmit information. It has three parts separated by dots: a header (token type and algorithm), a payload (user information called claims), and a signature (to verify the token hasn't been tampered with). You typically receive JWTs from authentication systems after login, or use them as access tokens in API requests.

items.q: Why decode a JWT?
items.a: Although tokens aren't encrypted, they are base64-encoded and unreadable as-is. Decoding a JWT lets you see the information it contains (issuer, expiration time, user ID, etc.) at a glance. During development, it's essential to verify that your token carries the right information and hasn't expired.

items.q: Is my token information safe here?
items.a: Yes, completely safe. This tool runs 100% in your browser. The JWT token or signing key you paste is never sent to a server and never leaves your device. All parsing and verification happen locally on your computer. Even in development, never paste production secret keys into untrusted tools.

items.q: When would I use a JWT decoder?
items.a: While developing or debugging an API, to check that the token in a login response or an Authorization header carries the right claims, to see whether a token has expired (exp), or to figure out the issuer, audience (aud), and permissions of a token you received from another service.

items.q: What should I watch out for with JWTs?
items.a: A JWT's payload is only base64-encoded, not encrypted, so anyone can read it — never put sensitive data like passwords in it. Don't trust tokens whose signature algorithm is 'none', as they're easy to forge. And it's safest not to paste production signing keys anywhere, including this tool.

items.q: Is it safe to paste my token here?
items.a: Yes, it's completely safe. This tool does all processing in your browser only. Your token and secret key are never sent anywhere or stored. Feel free to use it for testing during development.

items.q: What do I need to verify a signature?
items.a: For HMAC (HS256/384/512), you need the secret key used to create the signature. For RSA/ECDSA (RS256/ES256), you need the public key in PEM format. The public key is usually available from the issuer's authentication server via a JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) URL.

items.q: What algorithms does this tool support?
items.a: Currently, it supports HS256, HS384, HS512 (HMAC), RS256 (RSA), and ES256 (ECDSA). EdDSA (Ed25519) and other advanced algorithms are not yet supported.

items.q: What happens if my token is expired?
items.a: An expired token shows a red banner with '⛔ Expired' status. If the expiration time (exp claim) is earlier than the current time, it's expired. You can still verify the signature, but a real server would reject it.

items.q: What are claims?
items.a: Claims are pieces of information in the JWT payload. Standard claims like iss (issuer), sub (subject), exp (expiration), and iat (issued at) are defined by the JWT specification. Custom claims are application-specific information added as needed.

howTo.heading: How to use Everyone's Cheer
howTo.whatBody: Everyone's Cheer is a free online tool that shows a short cheer message in giant letters that fill the screen. At concerts, stadiums, and events you can wave your phone like an LED banner so your message is visible from far away. Choose a scrolling, flashing, or neon glow effect, set the text and background colors, adjust the size, rotate to landscape, and go fullscreen to build your own cheer banner. No app install and no sign-up — it runs right in your browser.
howTo.howBody: Type the message you want to show in the cheer message box and it appears instantly, large, in the preview above. You can also tap a suggested phrase for concerts, sports, birthdays, or events to fill it in fast. Then pick an effect and speed, choose text and background colors, and set the size to get the look you want; if you plan to hold your phone sideways, turn on landscape rotation. Finally, tap the fullscreen button and the whole browser screen becomes your cheer banner. Press Esc or tap the screen to exit.
howTo.useCasesBody: Show a fan message at your favorite artist's concert, or your team's chant at a baseball or soccer game. Display a birthday greeting at a party, or a fitting phrase at a school festival, company event, fan meeting, or proposal. It is also handy for signaling someone far away, or getting a message across in a loud place where words are hard to hear.
howTo.tipsBody: In dark venues, a black background with bright text (white, yellow, or coral) and the neon effect reads best. Use the scroll effect for longer messages and the flash effect to make short, punchy chants stand out. To keep the screen from turning off while you cheer, switch on “Keep screen on” (supported browsers only). If the text and background contrast is low a warning appears, so pick a high-contrast pair that stays readable from a distance.

items.q: Is my cheer message stored on a server?
items.a: No. Everything runs entirely in your browser and your message is never sent anywhere. Recent messages are saved only in this device’s browser storage (localStorage) for convenience, and you can clear them anytime.

items.q: Does it work offline?
items.a: Once the page has loaded, you can keep creating and showing cheer banners even if your connection drops. It is a 100% client-side tool that needs no server communication.

items.q: What effects are available?
items.a: Four effects: static (fixed), scroll (text glides across), flash (blinking), and neon (glowing text). Scroll and flash have adjustable speed — slow, medium, or fast.

items.q: How do fullscreen and landscape work?
items.a: Tap the fullscreen button to turn the whole browser screen into your banner for a bigger display. If you want to hold your phone sideways for extra width, turn on landscape and the text rotates 90 degrees to read horizontally.

items.q: Can I keep the screen from dimming while I cheer?
items.a: Turn on “Keep screen on” and the screen will not auto-dim or switch off while the banner is showing. This option appears only on browsers that support it (recent mobile Chrome, iOS Safari 16.4+, and others).

items.q: Does it work well on mobile?
items.a: Yes. Everyone's Cheer is built mobile-first for use at live venues. It is easy to operate one-handed and is responsive so the banner fills even small screens.

about.heading: About Jurepi
about.intro: Everyday tools, all in one place.

sections.title: Why we built it
sections.body: As the gap in access to AI's benefits keeps widening, we wanted to use that same technology to build tools that genuinely help everyone who visits—completely free. Tell us what tool or feature you need, and we'll build it and add it, one at a time.

sections.title: What is Jurepi?
sections.body: Jurepi is a free online tool hub designed to make everyday tasks simpler. Whether you need to make a fair decision for your group, keep a daily reflection journal, or find quick answers to common problems, Jurepi has you covered. No sign-up, no login, no hassle—just open a tool and get started.

sections.title: Privacy first
sections.body: We don't collect your personal information. When you use Jurepi tools, your data stays on your device. Everything you enter—names in the ladder game, reflections in the journal, calculations—stays safely in your browser's local storage and never leaves your computer. Your privacy is our priority.

sections.title: Tools for every need
sections.body: We start with tools that solve real problems: a fair lottery game for making group decisions, and a daily journal for self-reflection. More tools are coming soon across categories like math, text, conversions, and fun. Check back often as we expand what Jurepi can do for you.

sections.title: Why is it free?
sections.body: Every Jurepi tool is, and will stay, free. We cover our running costs with the ads shown on the page. To keep those ads from getting in the way, we reserve their space and height in advance so the page never jumps around—and we never sell your personal information. You can turn ad and analytics cookies off or on anytime via 'Privacy settings' in the footer.

sections.title: Built in the open
sections.body: Jurepi is built and run by one person—Jurepi, a developer who loves books. New tools, improvements, and notes on design and engineering are shared on the blog. Follow along to see how Jurepi grows.

sections.title: Built with AI, in the open
sections.body: Jurepi's tools and content are built with the help of modern AI—a way to turn ideas into working tools quickly and to ship useful features more often. The process and the result aren't hidden: the source code is public on GitHub, so anyone can see how a tool works and suggest improvements.

sections.title: How each tool is made
sections.body: Every tool starts from a real frustration. The math is verified with tests first, then checked by hand on real screens and phones before it goes live. When something's wrong, it gets fixed quickly rather than left alone. If you spot anything off while using a tool, let us know—that feedback is where the next improvement begins.

sections.title: Questions or ideas?
sections.body: If you have feedback on a tool, found a bug, or need a new feature, send an email anytime—we usually reply within a few days. Common questions and how-to notes are collected near the bottom of each tool page, so open a tool and take a look together.
